summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – CHILDREN – Where the respondent did not appear at the final hearing – Where the respondent did not file material in accordance with trial directions – Where final parenting orders were made as consented to by the applicant and the Independent Children’s Lawyer – Where the respondent is the biological mother – Where the applicant is the mother and primary carer – Where unacceptable risk – Where limited supervised time between the child and the respondent in child’s best interests.

subseq.zh: 标准层(自动摘要)。完整精读可后续升级。
subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

HARTNETT J

INTRODUCTION

These reasons for judgment support the making of final parenting orders on 30 January 2026.
The proceedings were initiated in the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2) on 15 November 2023, upon the applicant mother (“the applicant”) filing an Application for Final Parenting Orders in respect of the child, the subject of these proceedings, X born in 2020 (‘the child’). The child at trial was aged approximately five years. The respondent mother (“the respondent”) had filed a Response to Application for Final Parenting Orders together with a limited affidavit of evidence on 16 January 2024.
Orders made by Judge Bender on 12 December 2024 set the matter down for trial on 30 June 2025 and required, amongst other things, the respondent to file her trial material by 16 June 2025.
On 13 June 2025, the matter was transferred, pursuant to s 149 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia Act 2021 (Cth), to the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 1). The trial date was vacated.
On 8 July 2025, I made orders granting the respondent an extension for the filing of her trial documents to 15 October 2025 and set the matter down for trial on 27 January 2026. In a chambers order made 10 November 2025, that trial date was vacated and the trial listed to commence on 30 January 2026. The respondent, at that time, had still to file any material in accordance with the earlier orders requiring her to do so.
On 10 November 2025, the applicant filed an Application in a Proceeding seeking leave to proceed on an undefended basis.
On 18 December 2025, the applicant’s Application in a Proceeding filed 10 November 2025 proceeded before me at an interim defended hearing. The respondent was given leave to appear via video link and appeared as a litigant in person. The respondent made submissions as to her inability to secure representation pursuant to a s 102NA of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) order made by Judge Bender in the orders made by her on 12 December 2024. I determined to make a further s 102NA of the Act order and granted the respondent a further 14 days in which to file any affidavits of evidence and any other material on which she sought to rely at trial. The respondent again filed no material.
On 30 January 2026, the trial of the proceeding commenced. The respondent failed to appear. Counsel for the applicant made submissions as to why the matter should proceed undefended. She was supported in that application by counsel for the Independent Children’s Lawyer (“ICL”), an order for the appointment of an ICL being made on 22 January 2024. I granted the applicant leave to proceed on an undefended basis in circumstances where the respondent had failed to appear, and where the respondent had filed no trial affidavit(s) of evidence. I did, however, make an order, being Order 17, which provided for the respondent to seek to set aside the orders made on an undefended basis for a limited time only. I made that order because of the respondent’s current partner ringing the Court just prior to trial to leave a message that the respondent was ill.

ORDERS SOUGHT

The applicant sought orders as made by the Court on 30 January 2026, save to accommodate the views of the ICL, and have consent orders submitted to the Court on behalf of the ICL and the applicant, the applicant and the ICL agreed to: a continuation of the use of the parenting application known as AppClose, rather than a transition to a free application as sought by the ICL; and supervised time as between the respondent and the child occurring six times per year, rather than four time per year as originally sought by the applicant.

MATERIAL RELIED UPON

The applicant relied upon:
(1) the Further Further Amended Initiating Application filed 2 June 2025;

(2) the applicant’s trial affidavit filed 2 June 2025;

(3) the applicant’s Application in a Proceeding and affidavit filed 10 November 2025;

(4) the Family Report prepared by Ms H dated 24 March 2025;

(5) the applicant’s Case Outline filed 23 January 2026; and

(6) oral submissions as made by counsel.

The ICL relied upon:
(1) the affidavit of Ms J, Social Worker, at D Contact Service, Suburb C, dated 25 January 2025;

(2) the affidavits of Dr Sara Salessi filed 15 June and 30 September 2024 (annexing psychological evaluations of the applicant and the respondent respectively);

(3) the Family Report prepared by Ms H dated 24 March 2025; and

(4) oral submissions as made by counsel.

The respondent did not participate in the final hearing nor file any trial material in respect of that hearing as earlier referred to. The respondent had earlier sworn and filed affidavits on 5 December 2024, and 31 December 2024 (following the January 2024 affidavit). Neither provided a great deal of relevant factual material by the time of trial.

RELEVANT FACTUAL BACKGROUND

The applicant is 36 years of age and has been employed as a public servant since 2017. The applicant and the child live with the applicant’s mother, Ms L, who is aged 71 years. Ms L provides support to the applicant in the care of the child when necessary. The applicant does not wish to disclose her work, or her residential, address, given the history of family violence and coercion as perpetrated upon her by the respondent. That included physical assaults, gaslighting, lying, controlling behaviours and threats – not only of retaliatory and violent behaviours, but of enforced separation of the applicant and the child.
The respondent is also 36 years of age. She is originally from New Zealand. In the past, she has given her occupation as a tradesperson, and as a support worker, but according to Services Australia, she now claims to be unemployed. There is a current assessment for child support payable by her to the applicant of $145 per month. The respondent is not making that payment. The respondent resides with her current partner.
The parties were married in 2019. The applicant chose to change her surname to that of the respondent. They separated on 15 January 2021, when the child was an infant. Their relationship had spanned some three years. On 16 September 2022, the parties obtained final property orders by consent. Their divorce was finalised in October 2023.
The child of the relationship was conceived through a known donor in a private arrangement between the parties and the donor. The donor is of Country M background. The child was born in Melbourne in 2020. The respondent is the biological mother of the child. According to the birth certificate of the child, the parties are both registered as mother, and there is no entry for any other parent.
Following the child’s birth, the applicant took parental care leave until May 2021 as the respondent had intended to return to work after a time. During her leave, the applicant was, on a daily basis, extensively engaged in all aspects of the care of the child. The respondent was also involved in the care of the child until January 2021.
The respondent has two other children: a son, G, aged 13 years, who lives in New Zealand with his father; and a daughter, F aged 16 years, who had also been living with her and G’s father in New Zealand, but who had come to Melbourne to live with the parties in 2020.
The child commenced preparatory class in January this year at E School. The school is a very culturally diverse school which is appreciated by the applicant who would like the child to be exposed to many cultures and ideas. The child is in a classroom with his best friend from childcare, who also has Region Q heritage.

Family Violence

Each of the parties has obtained, in the past, intervention orders (“IVO”) against the other, which have now expired. The applicant sets out, in respect of her own actions, that she was charged with three persistent breaches of IVO against the respondent for which she received a six-month Good Behaviour Bond and no conviction. She had a consequent period of suspension in her workplace.
The applicant’s affidavit evidence otherwise sets out the extensive family violence and coercive control as perpetrated by the respondent upon the applicant, in relation to which the applicant obtained IVOs against the respondent who often sought a countering IVO against the applicant. The family violence of the respondent is corroborated by the Victoria Police material, which includes six separate family violence reports involving other intimate partners of the respondent that have the respondent as the aggressor; the Department of Families, Fairness and Housing material (“the Department”); and the respondent’s criminal record which includes five persistent breaches of an intervention order (to which the respondent pleaded guilty); as well as multiple charges in respect of an incident which occurred in late 2020 wherein, following a verbal altercation as between the applicant and the respondent, the respondent punched the applicant in the head repeatedly whilst the applicant was holding the child. The applicant managed to place the child on the ground, her mother picking the child up to secure the child’s safety, after which the respondent pulled the applicant to the ground and continued punching her, as well as kicking her. The respondent broke her hand punching the applicant. The applicant managed to get away and went to a neighbour’s house, taking the child with her, and as accompanied by the applicant’s mother. The respondent then entered the neighbour’s house where she cornered the applicant and the child, scratched at the applicant, and tore her earring out of her ear lobe, tearing her ear lobe in the process. The applicant’s mother called the ambulance and the police. The applicant was bleeding all over her body. The applicant required plastic surgery to fix her torn ear lobe. The applicant had further injuries sustained because of this assault including facial injuries. The Department became involved with the family, setting out a safety plan in August 2021. For the Department, one further part of the family history was the respondent’s then treatment of her child F. She was often verbally aggressive to her, and on one occasion hit her, causing F’s nose to bleed.
In late 2020, the respondent attempted suicide and remained in hospital overnight. The Department has a record of that event.
The respondent was found guilty of two charges in late 2024 but did not receive a conviction. She was instead sentenced to a community corrections order and unpaid community work. The respondent has appealed that guilty verdict and her appeal, earlier to be heard in mid-2025, is now listed to be heard in early 2026 for four days in the County Court of Victoria. The applicant is required to attend those proceedings, she being the victim, and go through yet another court hearing. Her mother and sister are also required to attend.
In January 2021, a further episode of family violence occurred, with the respondent again being the perpetrator, and the applicant the victim. In mid-January 2021, the applicant attempted to leave the family home with the child, as advised by the Department (Child Protection). Instead, the respondent offered to leave the family home and told the applicant to stay in the family home with the child. In late January 2021, the respondent returned to the family home despite advice from Child Protection that she was not to return to the home. The respondent proceeded to burn and damage some of the applicant’s belongings and damage the house. The respondent also attempted to remove the applicant’s watch from her wrist and otherwise attempted to intimidate the applicant. The applicant was forced to leave the family home to protect the child. The Department intervened thereafter and placed the child in the full-time care of the applicant where he remained until May 2021 when the parties, without objection from the Department, then agreed to share his care on a week-about basis, this arrangement being formalised by final consent orders on 3 June 2022 (where the parties were represented). That arrangement continued until October 2023, save for an occasion when the respondent travelled to New Zealand and left the child full-time with the applicant being in mid-2022.
During the period of shared care of the child, many difficulties arose in the parties’ communications about the child. It was a fraught period. On one occasion, the applicant conveyed to the respondent that the child had a short haircut because of the state of his hair and to prevent recurrence of his eczema condition. The respondent thereafter shaved the child’s head and continued to do so between January 2023 and October 2023, despite the applicant’s protests. In part, the respondent considered this a tradition of her cultural heritage. Otherwise, it was controlling and defiant behaviour intended to aggravate the applicant.
In mid-August 2023 the Department again became actively involved with the family because of the respondent’s unstable mental health/suicidal ideation, and family violence as occurring between the respondent and her then partner, Ms O, often in the presence of the child with such violence being physical on both sides. At that time, the respondent had also taken an overdose of medications and was in hospital. A further safety plan was constructed.
In October 2023, the Department again intervened in respect of the child’s care and placed him back into the full-time care of the applicant. On this occasion, the child’s safety had been compromised, and he had been subjected to family violence due to the respondent being in a violent relationship with her then-partner, to which the child was exposed. The child’s behaviour was deteriorating and the Department considered him in need of protection and placement with the applicant. That living arrangement has continued from October 2023 to January 2026 being the time of the trial.
Between October 2023 and March 2024, there was no contact between the child and the respondent, and the child’s behaviour improved. He was less dysregulated. He was being toilet trained. He had fewer outbursts. He was sleeping on his own. He was engaged in play therapy.
When time spent between the child and the respondent resumed in September 2024, the child’s ongoing behavioural issues at childcare, where he attended three times a week, and later at kindergarten, where he attended three times a week, were often remarked upon by his caregivers and needed to be addressed. The child would on occasion punch and choke both the applicant and other children. The applicant arranged for him to attend upon a psychologist in early 2025, with the child responding positively to his treatment.

Supervised Time

On 14 March 2024, at an interim defended hearing, Senior Judicial Registrar Crocker made orders suspending all previous parenting orders, which were the orders of 3 June 2022, and otherwise made orders including for the child to live with the applicant and spend supervised time with the respondent on a fortnightly basis at a contact centre. Further orders were made for the child to communicate with the respondent via FaceTime each Monday and Thursday from 4.30pm to 4.45pm, as well as each alternative Sunday between 2.00pm and 2.15pm (pending the commencement of supervised time).
Supervised time at the contact centre as between the child and the respondent did not commence until September 2024. There were 19 scheduled visits, but only 15 took place in the period between September 2024 and 29 June 2025, this last date being the last face-to-face visit between the child and the respondent. Those visits were of a one-and-a-half-hour duration. On the last visit, the respondent was accompanied by her other two children.
The visits ceased in June 2025, because D Contact Service, Suburb C advised that they could no longer facilitate fortnightly visits as between the respondent and the child. Court orders which made provision for the parties to sign further documentation for provision of ongoing supervision by N Community Centre were complied with by the applicant, but not the respondent. The applicant’s evidence as to the supervised visits which did occur was that such visits adversely impacted the child. She described him as often angry before the visits, not wanting to separate from her. She further described him as violent, dysregulated, throwing things around, screaming, and, often, coming into her bed in the evening for comfort, particularly after the visits with the respondent.
The ordered FaceTime calls, twice each week, have continued, with the respondent availing herself of the benefit of that order. It is the applicant’s evidence that those FaceTime calls have been problematic, with the respondent seeking to attack the applicant in front of the child, adversely impacting the child, and with the respondent, in her conversation and behaviours, not being child-focussed. For instance, the respondent’s response to the child telling her that he had recently started school was to show no real interest, albeit she asked him one or two questions. The child’s response to the respondent talking about herself during these times or being argumentative and combative with the applicant, both a regular occurrence, is to sometimes hang up; sometimes leave the room; and otherwise, to lash out at the applicant, punching and slapping her, to the expressed delight of the respondent. It is also the applicant’s evidence that the child is often dysregulated before and after the calls. The calls themselves are not lengthy, and even though there is a period of 15 minutes over which they can occur, they rarely extend beyond 5 to 10 minutes.

Psychological Evaluation by Dr Salessi

Dr Salessi is a clinical psychologist. She, as a single expert, conducted a psychological assessment of each of the parties.
In summary, her opinion was that the applicant has high-average intellectual abilities, no psychological disorder, and some anxiety proportionate to the stress occasioned by the circumstances – namely the proceeding. She opined that the applicant had shown strength and resilience in dealing with the various stressors that have arisen for her, and Dr Salessi had no concerns about the applicant having the care of the child and observed there to be no risk indicators. She recommended that the applicant engage in individual counselling.
The applicant followed that recommendation, having earlier sought out some assistance. This has included her attendance for family violence counselling with P Counselling Service; and counselling through the T Support Service in Suburb S. At trial, the applicant was waiting (having been placed on a waiting list) to engage in further counselling with R Support Service and U Support Service. She had completed several programs as set out in her affidavit evidence including a Parenting Orders Program; Tuning in to Kids program; and a post separation parenting course. She has been pro-active in seeking out any necessary or beneficial professional supports and will continue to be so engaged.
In summary, Dr Salessi’s opinion as to the respondent was that she has average-range intellectual ability, shows some personality vulnerabilities, and needed to be assessed through longitudinal treatment given the history as provided by the respondent to Dr Salessi, which Dr Salessi considered needed further investigation and diagnosis. In that regard, the respondent reported to Dr Salessi in September 2024, that she had tumours in her brain and throat; that she has a history of anxiety and depression; and that she has been diagnosed with the following conditions: complex PTSD (2021); autism spectrum disorder (2023); and ADHD (2024). Dr Salessi detailed that complex trauma history as provided by the respondent, and opined that the respondent had emotional dysregulation, poor coping, turbulent interpersonal relationships, susceptibility to depression/anxiety, and the possibility of being on the spectrum of autism, possibly ADHD. I observe that in the subpoenaed medical documents relating to the respondent’s treatments and attendances on hospitals and doctors, there is no confirmation of any diagnosis of autism or ADHD. Dr Salessi also expressed concern about the respondent’s impulsivity, her disinhibition, her aggression, her difficulty in being child-focussed, and the allegations of family violence as levelled against her, not just by the applicant, but also by the father of the respondent’s older children, and by an ex-partner of the respondent.
In the opinion of Dr Salessi, if the Court were to make an order for supervised time spent, any supervised time between the child and the respondent should be professionally supervised. The respondent’s observed lack of ability to be child-focussed significantly concerned Dr Salessi, who recommended that the respondent undergo individual therapy and adhere to any medication regime to address that issue. At trial the Court had no evidence before it as to the respondent addressing any recommendations of the single expert.

Family Report

The family report writer, Ms H, saw the parties separately on 17 January 2025, and saw the child, and subsequently observed each party with the child. Her report is dated 24 March 2025. She made a number of recommendations, which appear as part of the orders as sought by the applicant and the ICL, save for the suggestion of a parenting co-ordinator which the parties cannot afford and which in my view, is not appropriate in the circumstances of this case, and one important recommendation, namely that the child spend supervised time with the respondent fortnightly.
Ms H’s recommendation for fortnightly supervised time was predicated on the fact that the respondent had then an incurable terminal illness, as conveyed to her by the respondent. The respondent claimed that she has been diagnosed with a terminal, inoperable brain cancer; that she suffers from tumours in her brain and neck; and that she has three to five years left to live.
That contextual circumstance, the respondent’s impending death, as claimed by her, “I’m going to be dead in three to five years,” significantly troubled the family report writer. She said to the respondent, “Well, what kind of plan have you got? How’s [X] going to cope, seeing you deteriorating, maybe going into palliative care,” to which the respondent failed to reply, a matter noted by the report writer. Her recommendation that the child spend fortnightly supervised time with the respondent was informed by the respondent’s false claim. The respondent had lied. The reality is that the respondent has provided no probative evidence of any incurable terminal illness, be it cancer or other illness.
I observe, in the context of the respondent’s claims as to her terminal illness, the Court made orders on 18 December 2025, including, relevantly, the below:
By 12.00pm on 19 December 2025, the respondent mother provide in writing to the solicitor for the applicant mother, and to the Independent Children’s Lawyer, a complete list of the names of all medical practitioners, including any psychologists and/or psychiatrists, upon whom she has attended in the past four years, to also include any hospital attendances and/or admissions in that period.
The respondent complied with the above order, but in the list provided by her she listed hospitals and other entities, and private practitioners who had no records relating to her. Her actions resulted in the wastage of significant Commonwealth expense in the issuing of, and responding to, subpoenas. Counsel for the applicant and Counsel for the ICL examined some 1500 pages of medical files relating to the respondent as subpoenaed during the proceeding, including those responsive to the December 2025 order. The respondent appears to have a medical syndrome affecting the eye. She has otherwise not that which she claimed to the family report writer.
It is also noteworthy and of concern in terms of the child’s exposure to the respondent, and the risks it presents, that the respondent has no hesitation in also lying to medical professionals as to her health history. For example, on 19 December 2023, the respondent told V Hospital that she had a medical syndrome, and a tumour in her brain and throat diagnosed in October 2022. Only three months earlier in 2023, the respondent had told V Hospital that she had not had diagnostic imaging. The documents produced by W Medical Centre included a scan of a part of the respondent’s anatomy, as referred by the treating medical professional, of 21 November 2022, which also pointed to the respondent’s disregard of the truth, as did the October 2022 records, both set out below. The results of the November 2022 scan described:
There is no neurological cause for her [...], and it was a completely normal brain and neck and apex of the lung.
The October 2022 MRI and MRA undergone by the respondent concluded:
No cause of [the respondent’s medical syndrome] is identified intracranially. Normal basis skull and neck. Normal neck vessels. No evidence of a carotid dissection.
On 16 April 2024, at the request of the respondent, her general practitioner referred her for further examination. She underwent a CT scan. That result was:
CT: brain. Conclusion: unremarkable study.
Additionally, the respondent lied to the family report writer in her claim to be of Torres Strait Islander heritage, and in her further claim that the child has such a connection. Indeed, the respondent had used the services of the Victorian Aboriginal Child and Community Agency (“VACCA”) to place the child in an Aboriginal childcare facility. The child’s attendance ceased when VACCA could find no evidence of the child’s Aboriginality. On the evidence, the respondent has no such heritage, and nor does the child.
Finally, the respondent told the family report writer that the child had autism and ADHD traits. The applicant, who had not made any such claim, nor had cause to, nevertheless responded to that allegation by having the child attend upon a paediatrician on three occasions for the purposes of testing for such conditions. The child is not autistic and nor does he have ADHD.

LEGAL PRINCIPLES

Parental responsibility is dealt with in Division 2 of Pt VIII of the Act. Relevantly, s 61CA of the Act provides as follows:
Consultation between parents on major long - term issues

If it is safe to do so, and subject to any court orders, the parents of a child who is not yet 18 are encouraged:
(a) to consult each other about major long - term issues in relation to the child; and

(b) in doing so, to have regard to the best interests of the child as the paramount consideration.

(Emphasis in original)
Relevantly, s 61D of the Act provides as follows:
Parenting orders and parental responsibility
(1) A parenting order confers parental responsibility for a child on a person, but only to the extent to which the order confers on the person duties, powers, responsibilities or authority in relation to the child.

(2) A parenting order in relation to a child does not take away or diminish any aspect of the parental responsibility of any person for the child except to the extent (if any):

(a) expressly provided for in the order; or

(b) necessary to give effect to the order.
(3) A parenting order that deals with the allocation of responsibility for making decisions about major long - term issues in relation to the child (see subsection 64B(3)) may provide for joint or sole decision - making in relation to all or specified major long - term issues.
(Emphasis in original)
Pursuant to s 60CA of the Act:
In deciding whether to make a particular parenting order in relation to a child, a court must regard the best interests of the child as the paramount consideration.
To determine the best interests of the child, I must have regard to the considerations in the Act as set out in ss 60CC(1), 60CC(2) and 60CC(2A) of the Act. Those sections provide, relevantly:
How a court determines what is in a child’s best interests

...

(1) ...in determining what is in the child's best interests, the court must:
(a) consider the matters set out in subsection (2);
General considerations
(2) For the purposes of paragraph (1)(a), the court must consider the following matters:
(a) what arrangements would promote the safety (including safety from being subjected to, or exposed to, family violence, abuse, neglect, or other harm) of:
(i) the child; and
(ii) each person who has care of the child (whether or not a person has parental responsibility for the child);
(b) any views expressed by the child;
(c) the developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs of the child;

(d) the capacity of each person who has or is proposed to have parental responsibility for the child to provide for the child’s developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs;

(e) the benefit to the child of being able to have a relationship with the child’s parents, and other people who are significant to the child, where it is safe to do so;

(f) anything else that is relevant to the particular circumstances of the child.

(2A) In considering the matters set out in paragraph (2)(a), the court must include consideration of:
(a) any history of family violence, abuse or neglect involving the child or a person caring for the child (whether or not the person had parental responsibility for the child); and

(b) any family violence order that applies or has applied to the child or a member of the child’s family.

(Emphasis in original)
When considering the risk of family violence, the following is set out in s 60CG of the Act:
(1) In considering what order to make, the court must, to the extent that it is possible to do so consistently with the child’s best interests being the paramount consideration, ensure that the order:
(a) is consistent with any family violence order; and

(b) does not expose a person to an unacceptable risk of family violence.
(2) For the purposes of paragraph (1)(b), the court may include in the order any safeguards that it considers necessary for the safety of those affected by the order.
In considering these matters, the Court must give consideration as to whether there is an unacceptable risk of family violence and/or an inability to promote the safety of a child and parent in the making of parenting orders also involving a consideration of unacceptable risk. Upon establishing the existence of an unacceptable risk, the Court must then determine whether that risk “is able to be sufficiently managed or ameliorated”.[1]
The Court is required to assess its factual findings on the balance of probabilities, and the impact of those findings upon the assessment of future risk. Accordingly, Isles & Nelissen [2022] FedCFamC1A 97; (2022) 65 Fam LR 288 is applicable. The Court’s determination as to what did or did not happen in the past is determined on the balance of probabilities. However, in hypothesising about future possibilities the Court is required to engage in an assessment of risk as a predictive exercise.[2] That assessment of risk is informed by the factual findings as to the past.

CONSIDERATION

There is no current operative final IVO as between the parties or involving the child as a protected person.
The best interests of the child are served by the applicant having sole parental responsibility for making decisions about major long-term issues in relation to the child; by the child living with the applicant; and by the child spending only supervised and limited periods of time with the respondent at the present time. They are the arrangements that would promote the safety of the child and the applicant.
The child is a young child, who was too young to express a view to the family report writer. He has just commenced school. His primary carer, in large part throughout his life, has been, and continues to be, the applicant. The child depends on her to both be, and feel, secure. He has all his needs met by the applicant, including his emotional, financial, physical, medical, and psychological needs. He has not seen the respondent since June 2025, and prior to then saw her in a supervised setting. Those numerous supervised visits were generally successful, and happy times for the child and the respondent. There was clearly a loving bond between the two, a lot of laughter, as there was between the child and G, and the child and F, the child’s half-siblings whom the applicant permitted the attendance of on one occasion.
Unfortunately however, the respondent also used a visit in October 2024, to tell the child that his “Papa” had died, which confused the child until the mother said it was her father who had died (the child did not think he had a father); and a visit in December 2024, and one further visit thereafter, to tell the child that he had a father. She showed no insight into the impact upon the child of being given that information. The child having a father was not something ever before raised by the parties with the child, the donor having agreed not to be involved in the life of the child and the parties having a written agreement to not inform the child of his donor father’s conception and identity until he was older. It was clearly a fact which required a careful and timely approach by the parties, commensurate with the child’s maturity and age, and particularly with the applicant’s primary input. The issue was one the respondent was intent on disclosing to the child without her consulting in any way with the applicant. Further, the respondent was intent on, as she described to Dr Salessi, including the child’s donor father in his life, a matter she had not consulted the applicant about, and a matter not agreed between the parties and the donor. Her claim that she wished to ensure that the child has connections to his Country M culture, I infer, was a matter that she considered absent in the applicant’s approach to parenting. In fact, I find that the promotion of the cultural heritage of the child has been foremost in the mind and actions of the applicant. She is desirous of promoting, and does promote, the child’s Country Y heritage; Country M heritage; and connections to the respondent’s cultural heritage.
It was the applicant’s evidence that the supervised visits essentially caused the child emotional turmoil both before and after they occurred, as described above. The supervision evidence indicated some confusion for the child as to why he could not go to the respondent’s home, and as to why the parties could not see each other. At times he did not want to go to the visits. At other times, he was very excited. The respondent made no arrangements for the continuation of supervised time after the contact centre could no longer offer its services to the parties, and time ceased. The phone calls continued.
The child has continued to thrive in the care of the applicant. The child has no neurodiversity or trauma-related issues.[3] The cessation of disruptive and adverse (to the child’s well-being) FaceTime calls should further promote his best interests.
The respondent’s distant acquaintance with the truth has been very problematic for the child, and for the applicant, particularly the respondent’s claims about her state of health. V Hospital exchanged information with The Orange Door in December 2023, with both institutions expressing concern about the respondent’s “long history of untruths”. The Orange Door at that time informed the hospital that the respondent had perpetrated significant family violence towards two known partners, resulting in children being removed from the home, one of which was obviously the applicant.
The respondent has no insight into the consequences of her anti-social behaviours; her untreated mental health issues; nor the consequences of her perpetration of family violence upon the applicant and child.
Dr Salessi, in interview with the respondent, noted that the respondent did not have anything positive to say about the applicant’s parenting and continued to criticise her throughout the interview. Indeed, the respondent’s criticism of the applicant continues unabated and regardless of whether the child can hear that criticism and abuse of the applicant.
The Department has twice removed the child from the care of the respondent because of safety issues for the child in the respondent’s care. The respondent nevertheless maintained in her earlier material filed that there is no risk to the child in her care. On the evidence, I find that the risk does exist and is unacceptable. It can only be ameliorated by the putting in place of limited, supervised time. The respondent has done nothing to address her vulnerabilities, psychological issues, and propensity for violence, and has not responded to any suggestions of attending upon a psychologist or psychiatrist or other allied health professional in respect of her interaction with the child and the applicant to improve those interactions.
Ultimately, the Court made orders as sought by those parties who participated in this proceeding which was undefended by the respondent, and the unchallenged material on which the applicant and ICL relied supported the making of orders as sought by them.
